Q1: My study routine was honestly pretty unconventional. I couldn't afford to attend classes regularly because I work part-time to support myself, so I had to be strategic about when I studied. I'd usually carve out time late at night or early morning when my shift was done, and I'd focus intensely on the material that mattered most for exams. I'm a hands-on learner, so I'd practice problems and work through examples rather than just reading. It wasn't structured in a traditional sense, but it worked for me.
Q2: My pattern varied a lot depending on my work schedule and deadlines. Some weeks I could barely squeeze in any study time, and then I'd have to make up for it by cramming before exams. When assignments were due, I'd prioritize those, but honestly, I couldn't always complete them because of time constraints. I tried to be consistent, but life doesn't always cooperate when you're juggling multiple responsibilities.
Q3: Usually I'd log in when I absolutely needed something specific—like checking assignment requirements or downloading lecture materials to study offline. Sometimes I'd log in to see if there were any announcements I'd missed since I wasn't in class. The LMS was more of a resource hub for me than a place I'd hang out. I also logged in when peers reached out about group discussions.
Q4: Honestly, the LMS never really became a daily habit for me. I used it when necessary, but I couldn't afford to spend a lot of time online browsing through everything. What did become routine was downloading materials in bulk when I had good internet access, then working through them offline. The peer support group I was part of helped remind me to check in sometimes, which was nice.
Q5: The video lectures were actually really helpful when I could watch them—I'd often watch them at 1.5x speed to save time. The discussion posts were useful too because connecting with other students helped me understand concepts I'd missed from not attending class. I found that anything I could download and work through hands-on was most valuable because that matches how I learn best.
Q6: I often skipped the optional readings and supplementary materials because I just didn't have time. When you're working and studying with limited resources, you have to prioritize ruthlessly. I also didn't engage much with some of the interactive features because my internet access at home isn't always reliable. I focused on what would directly impact my grade.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time if it directly connected to exam content or helped me understand a difficult concept. I also valued anything that gave me practical skills I could apply. If something felt like busywork or wasn't clearly connected to my learning goals, I'd skip it. Time is precious when you don't have much of it.
Q8: Optional activities felt like luxuries I couldn't afford. Required ones got done because they had to, even if I was exhausted. With optional stuff, I'd think about whether it would actually help me understand the material better or improve my grade—if not, I'd let it go. It's not that I didn't want to engage more; I just had to be realistic about my capacity.
Q9: Absolutely, most of my studying happened offline. I'd download PDFs, lecture slides, and any materials I could access, then study them on my phone during breaks at work or late at night without internet. I also borrowed textbooks from the library and took handwritten notes that I'd review repeatedly. My actual learning happened way more outside the LMS than inside it.
Q10: There were so many times I was deeply engaged with the material but the LMS wouldn't know. I'd be on the bus reviewing notes I'd written out by hand, or I'd be at work during a slow moment mentally working through problems. Sometimes I'd study with a peer in person, going over concepts together. None of that shows up in any system.
Q11: Yes, this was basically my main strategy. I'd log in once, download everything I needed for the week or even the whole unit, and then work through it offline at my own pace. My internet isn't always stable, so this was the most practical approach. I probably watched each video multiple times offline, but the system only saw me access it once.
Q12: There were definitely hard moments, especially mid-semester when work got busier and I was feeling really overwhelmed. The stress was intense, and I felt sad a lot because I wanted to do better but circumstances kept getting in the way. Missing so many classes made me feel disconnected, and sometimes I wondered if I even belonged at university. The peer support really helped during those times.
Q13: All of those factors affected me constantly. Fatigue was a big one—working and studying meant I was always tired. Sometimes I'd open the LMS but be too exhausted to actually absorb anything. Competing responsibilities meant I had to choose between studying and earning money for rent. Confusion happened when I'd miss foundational content from not attending class.
Q14: Yes, there were times I'd have the LMS open but my mind was elsewhere. I'd be so tired that I'd read the same paragraph multiple times without it sinking in. Or I'd start a video and realize ten minutes later that I hadn't processed any of it. Being physically present online doesn't mean being mentally engaged.
Q15: I postponed assignments more than I wanted to. It wasn't that I didn't know they were important—I did. But when you're choosing between completing an assignment and working a shift that pays your bills, sometimes the assignment loses. I felt guilty about it, but survival comes first. I always tried to make up for it by doing well on exams.
Q16: Deadlines were the main thing that drove my LMS activity. I'd log in more frequently as deadlines approached, trying to make sure I understood what was required. The pressure was stressful, but it also forced me to prioritize. Without deadlines, I probably would have engaged even less with the platform because I'd just study offline.
Q17: When I got good feedback, it motivated me to keep pushing despite everything. Knowing that my exam performance was strong helped me believe I could succeed even with my unconventional approach. Negative feedback on assignments I couldn't complete properly was discouraging, but I tried to learn from it. The peer support group helped me process feedback constructively.
Q18: The course structure was challenging for someone in my situation. It seemed designed for students who could attend regularly and complete everything on time. I had to adapt it to fit my reality, which meant focusing on high-stakes assessments where I could prove my understanding. The flexibility to access materials online was helpful, but the attendance expectations didn't work for me.
Q19: Having materials available for download made engagement possible for me. That was crucial. But the expectation of regular attendance and consistent assignment completion made it harder—those weren't realistic for my circumstances. If there had been more flexibility in how students could demonstrate their learning, I might have engaged differently with the formal requirements.
Q20: I think some students might be searching for what they need, trying to understand the system, or feeling anxious about the work. High activity doesn't always mean productive activity. They might also be struggling with the material and spending time trying to figure things out without successfully completing tasks. Or life circumstances might be preventing them from finishing what they start.
Q21: That completely reflects my experience. My LMS activity was low, but I was learning constantly—just not in ways the system could track. I studied offline, I worked through problems by hand, I discussed concepts with peers in person. My exam score shows I understood the material deeply. The LMS just couldn't see how I was engaging.
Q22: Course logs can show when and how often someone logs in, what they click on, and what they submit. But they miss the depth of understanding, the offline work, the mental effort, and the personal circumstances affecting engagement. They can't tell if someone is truly learning or just going through the motions. They also can't capture the emotional journey of being a student.
Q23: Being engaged means genuinely caring about understanding the material and putting in effort to learn, even when it's hard. For me, it meant finding ways to master content despite missing classes and having limited time. It's about being mentally invested in your education, not just physically present in a system. True engagement is internal, not just behavioral.
Q24: The LMS wouldn't show how hard I fought to succeed despite my circumstances. It wouldn't show the nights I stayed up studying after exhausting work shifts, or the emotional weight of feeling like I was always behind. It wouldn't capture the peer support that kept me going, or the resilience it took to earn a Distinction while barely attending class. My data looks like disengagement, but my reality was anything but.
